coredata.dc:title: Highly effective recognition of Fe3+ and lysine based on hydrosoluble N-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ullah Khan, Waheed
Muzammal Hussain, Muhammad
Xu, Weiqing
Zeng, Yating
Ahmed, Farid
Xiong, Hai
coredata.dc:description: In this study, dual-color nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized using a simple solvothermal method. These fluorescent NCDs displayed excitation-independent emission in red (598 nm in ethanol) and orange (553 nm in water) due to their effect of two different solvents. Both types of NCDs exhibited high stability under UV irradiation for 80 min. The water-soluble NCDs showed excellent sensitivity to Fe3+ ions by the quenching of their photoluminescent (PL) intensity. Quantitative analysis revealed a gradual decline in PL intensity, with a low detection limit of approximately 0.97 µM. Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence of NCDs caused by Fe3+ could be restored by the addition of lysine to the (NCDs + Fe3+) solution, with an estimated detection limit of about 0.37 µM. This phenomenon could be explained in the terms of non-radiative electron/hole recombination, leading to aggregation through efficient photo-induced electron transfer (PET). Utilizing their high selectivity and sensitivity, these NCDs served as “On-Off-On” switches and were successfully employed for the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples, contributing to environmental safety and control efforts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-color fluorescence
Sensors
Fe3+ detection
Lysine recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of NCDs toward Fe3+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Fields of Universities in Guangdong Province
Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen, China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen , China (JCYJ20210324095607021 to HX), the Special Project of Key Fields of Universities in Guangdong Province, China ( 2021ZDZX2047 to HX).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the synthesis of red and orange emitting NCDs by solvothermal approach. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) HR-TEM image of the NCDs and (inset) magnified image with lattice fringes. (b) Size histogram of NCDs. (c) XRD profile, and (d) FT-IR spectroscopy of NCDs.
(a) Full XPS spectrum of NCDs. (b) High-resolution C1s spectrum. (c) N1s Spectrum. (d) O1s spectrum.

(a) UV–vis spectrum and (b) PL excitation and excitation independent emission spectra of red emissive NCDs (c) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectrum of orange emissive NCDs. (d) Photo-luminescent excitation-independent emission spectra of water-soluble orange emissive NCDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Metal ions' effect on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs. (b) Changes in the PL emission of NCDs with increasing concentrations of Fe3+. (c) Linear plot of F/F0 against the concentration of Fe3+. (d) Interfering of various bio-analytes to the NCDs + Fe3+ solution and recording the PL spectra. (e) PL spectra were examined upon various amounts of lysine (1.25–50 µM). (f) F/F0 plotted against concentration (inset) linear relationship between F/F0 and lysine concentration.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs with Fe3+ and lysine. (b) Decay time spectra of NCDs and with Fe3+ and lysine. (c) Graphical representation of ON-OFF-ON mechanism of NCDs in the presence of Fe3+ and Lysine.
Comparison of NCDs with previously reported CDs for the detection of Fe3+.
Comparison of NCDs with previously reported CDs for the detection of Lysine.
Analytical performance of NCDs in tap/lake water samples.

head.ce:title: Highly effective recognition of Fe3+ and lysine based on hydrosoluble N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Study, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Study, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, dual-color nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized using a simple solvothermal method. These fluorescent NCDs displayed excitation-independent emission in red (598 nm in ethanol) and orange (553 nm in water) due to their effect of two different solvents. Both types of NCDs exhibited high stability under UV irradiation for 80 min. The water-soluble NCDs showed excellent sensitivity to Fe3+ ions by the quenching of their photoluminescent (PL) intensity. Quantitative analysis revealed a gradual decline in PL intensity, with a low detection limit of approximately 0.97 µM. Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence of NCDs caused by Fe3+ could be restored by the addition of lysine to the (NCDs + Fe3+) solution, with an estimated detection limit of about 0.37 µM. This phenomenon could be explained in the terms of non-radiative electron/hole recombination, leading to aggregation through efficient photo-induced electron transfer (PET). Utilizing their high selectivity and sensitivity, these NCDs served as “On-Off-On” switches and were successfully employed for the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples, contributing to environmental safety and control efforts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their myriad applications, exceptional photoluminescent characteristics, nanoscale size, and tunable emission [1–4]. Various fluorescent nanomaterials, such as semiconductor quantum dots [5,6], optical nano-clusters [7,8], rare-earth emissive products [9,10], etc., have recently undergone extensive investigation. However, the presence of poisonous and heavy metals (Pb, Cs, Cd, Zn), along with their low water solubility and poor stability, has prevented the wide use of these materials in sensing and medicine domains. Carbon dots (CDs) are remarkable fluorescent nanomaterials that have gained substantial attention across various disciplines. This is attributed to their unique physical and chemical characteristics including facile preparation, cost-effectiveness, abundant carbon sources, ultra-small size, wide absorption spectrum, strong fluorescence, multicolor emission, high water solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent resistance to photo-bleaching, and chemical inertness [11–15]. In addition to these outstanding physiochemical properties, they find widespread use in promising applications such as chem-bio probing [16], pH and temperature sensing [12,17], drug delivery [18,19], catalysis [20,21], bioimaging and diagnosis [22–25]. Despite these excellent features, CDs still lack the ability to produce long-wavelength and multicolor emissive products, limiting their practical applications.

Upon thorough examination of the existing literature, it has been discovered that a wide range of materials and techniques have been employed in the synthesis of CDs [26]. The documented processes include laser ablation, pyrolysis, electrochemical oxidation, hydro/solvothermal reactions, and microwave treatment [27]. The reported raw materials range from naturally occurring products to compounds generated in laboratories. However, only a small percentage of the resulting CDs are found to emit at longer wavelengths; the majority emit in the blue and blue-green spectra [22,28,29]. Furthermore, one of the main drawbacks of CDs is their inability to produce emissive products with long wavelengths (>500 nm). Therefore, to improve the luminescence properties and expand the range of applications, it is imperative to develop CDs that can emit light beyond the blue region. These long-wavelength emissive CDs have the potential for chemical and biosensing applications, which are primarily for growing concerns about environmental safety and the significant impact on the health of both humans and animals [30,31].

Fe3+ is a transition metal and plays a vital role in the human body, contributing significantly to various biological processes, including oxygen transport in tissues, energy production, and DNA synthesis [28]. Both low and high concentrations of Fe3+ can be detrimental to human health, affecting immunity [32]. However, an excessive concentration of Fe3+ can damage cells and lead to various disorders, such as hepatitis, heart attacks, and anemia [33]. Recently, the detection of long-wavelength emissive CDs are rarely reported to probe heavy and toxic metal (Fe3+) ions. For instance, Wang et al. developed dual emissive CDs exhibiting intense emission at 460/562 nm for detecting Fe3+ ions, although the limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 9.3/15.7 µM [28]. Additionally, green emissive CDs were established using diammonium hydrogen citrate and urea, with a particle size of 3.1 nm, for probing Fe3+ [34]. However, these reported CDs have a relatively high detection limit of about 19 µM. Various other studies have reported similar findings but have been impeded by poor sensitivity, high LOD, and a non-linear response, limiting their suitability for sensing applications. Therefore, it is crucial to develop precise and rapid methods for probing Fe3+ levels to accurately monitor its concentration and prevent the above-mentioned dangerous diseases. On the other hand, lysine is an essential amino acid regarded as one of the fundamental building blocks of proteins. It is essential for tissue growth, protein production, the strengthening of the immune system, and helping in the repair of the human body. Long-term use of lysine can cases stomach pain and diarrhea [35]. To the best of our knowledge, only a few studies have been reported to investigate the effects of lysine so far [36]. Recently, a CDs-based system has been developed for the on–off-on recognition of Sn2+ and lysine [37]. However, the sensing capacity of these CDs is quite low toward lysine, approximately 3.44 µM. Consequently, it is crucial for environmental safety and human/animal health concerns to develop a sensitive system to trace metal ions (Fe3+) along with biomolecules (lysine) in an aqueous medium with a low limit of detection.

Herein, we designed long-wavelength emissive (red and orange) NCDs through solvothermal approach using o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) and ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (EDA-2HCl) (Fig. 1 ). The two precursors (o-PDA/EDA-2HCl) has been utilized as carbon and nitrogen sources respectively, aiming to produced long-wavelength emissive CNDs and having chem/bio sensing capability. The resulting NCDs were well-characterized using various techniques, including HR-TEM, XPS, FT-IR, and XRD. The photoluminescent properties of the red and orange NCDs were assessed using fluorescence, UV–vis absorption, and PL decay spectrometry. The water-soluble orange-emissive CDs were quenched upon the addition of Fe3+ ions to the solution and thus were recovered upon the addition of a biomolecule (lysine). The findings demonstrated that the Y-CDs exhibited good selectivity towards metal ions (Fe3+) and lysine, with a limit of detection as low as 0.97 µM and 0.37 µM, respectively. Furthermore, the newly synthesized Y-CDs-based fluorescent probe was successfully employed to detect Fe3+ in real samples.

o-PDA, EDA-2HCl, ethanol, ascorbic acid, ampicillin sodium, chloramphenicol, cysteine, dopamine, glucose, glutathione (GSH), l-methionine, arginine, histidine, lysine, and all the salts (NaCl, FeSO4, NiCl2, CuCl, AlCl3, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, RuCl3, PbCl2, MnCl2, HgSO4, CdCl2, CaCl2, AgNO3, FeCl3, LiCl were obtained from Macklin industries. All the chemicals/salts were used without purification.

In detail, 1 g of o-PDA and 0.5 g of EDA-2HCl were mixed in 35 mL absolute ethanol. The mixture was sonicated for 20 min and then transferred into an autoclave for heating at 200 ℃ for 16 h. Subsequently, the mixture was collected at room temperature and purified via a 0.22 µm filter. The resulting pure mixture was dried using a rotary evaporation system, and the obtained powder was used for further characterization. For luminescent property monitoring, 5 mg of CDs powder was dispersed in 45 mL of water and ethanol, filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane, and used throughout for study. The characterization details and general procedure for the analyte are provided in the supporting information.

The morphology and surface analysis of the CDs were examined, as depicted in Fig. 2 (a-d). The TEM image revealed the presence of nanoparticles of the NCDs, with particles evenly distributed and exhibited spherical shapes, as illustrated in Fig. 2a. The fringe distance of the NCDs was calculated at ∼0.28 nm (inset of Fig. 2a), corresponding to a 100-lattice spacing of graphitic planes [38,39]. HRTEM image can be find in supporting information Fig. S1. The average particle size of the CDs was determined and illustrated in Fig. 2b. Measurements conducted on over 120 particles revealed a particle diameter ranging from 4 nm to 11 nm, with an average size of 7.4 nm. The structure and surface chemistry of the synthesized NCDs were further investigated using XRD and FT-IR spectroscopy techniques. The phase purity of the CDs was assessed by XRD in the range of 10-80° (Fig. 2c). The XRD pattern exhibited a broad peak centered at 24.1°, corresponding to the (002) facet of graphitic carbons [40,41]. FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to investigate the surface chemistry of the NCDs, along with the starting materials, including (o-PDA and EDA-2HCl) as a reference (Fig. 2d). The FT-IR spectrum revealed a broad peak-ranging from 3550 to 2700 cm−1, which can be ascribed to OH/NH2/CH surface groups, enhancing solubility and stability [42]. The CO group and CN bands were characterized by vibration bands at 1621 and 1120 cm−1 , respectively [43]. The bands at 1521 and 1237 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CC and CO, respectively [44]. At the lower frequency of the FT-IR spectrum, a CH band was observed at 745 cm−1. The findings suggest that the surface of the NCDs is covered by various functional groups.

The surface characteristics of the NCDs were analyzed using XPS. The results demonstrated that the composition of the NCDs primarily consists of C, N, O, and Cl, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The atomic percentages of the basic elements, namely C1s, N1s, O1s, and Cl2p, were 74.3 %, 20.1 %, 2.97 %, and 2.73 %, respectively, as illustrated inset Fig. 3a of the supporting information. The C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks associated with CC/CC (284.2 eV), CN/CO (285.1 eV), and CN (286.0 eV), as mentioned in Fig. 3b [45]. Similarly, the N1s high-resolution spectrum exhibited three discernible peaks (Fig. 3c), corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, observed at 398.8 eV, 399.6 eV, and 401.1 eV, respectively [46]. Furthermore, Fig. 3d presents the O1s spectrum, which revealed the presence of CO/OH (531.9 eV) and OCO (533.0 eV) functional groups on the surface of the NCDs [47]. The XPS data provide evidence that the CDs’ core was functionalized with N and O functional groups.

Long-wavelength fluorescent CDs are gaining increasing attention due to their infrequent occurrence in the literature. In this study, we developed red and orange emissive NCDs using a simple solvothermal method. The NCDs dispersed in ethanol show red emission, while those dispersed in water exhibit orange emission, as displayed in Fig. 4 . The UV–vis absorption spectra of red NCDs reveal distinct absorption peaks centered at 245 nm and 300 nm, corresponding to π-π* interactions of CC bonds. The absorbance in the lower energy spectrum (350–700 nm) is attributed to conjugated CO and CN groups, associated with n-π transitions (Fig. 4a). PL excitations and emission of the red NCDs are examined and depicted in Fig. 4b. PL excitation reveals a maximum peak at around 540 nm, with emission monitoring at 598 nm. The highest emission from the red emissive NCDs is achieved with a 540 nm excitation source. The NCDs demonstrate excitation-independent emission, with the most intense emission occurring at 598 nm and a full-width half maximum (FWHM) of 33 nm, notably narrower compared to previously reported works [42,48]. NCDs dispersed in water emit light in the orange region. The UV–vis absorption spectrum, with PL excitation and emission data, is presented in Fig. 4c-d. The UV–vis absorption spectra exhibit obvious absorption peaks at 234 nm, 288 nm, and 420 nm, corresponding to π-π* and n-π transitions of CC, CO, and conjugated CO/CN bonds, respectively [49]. The PL excitation reveals a maximum excitation peak of approximately 415 nm, with a monitoring emission wavelength of 553 nm, as demonstrated in Fig. 4c. Remarkably, the NCDs exhibit excitation-independent emission, peaking at 553 nm (Fig. 4d). The FWHM is determined to be 73 nm under 415 nm excitation. The excitation-independent emission phenomenon of both NCDs can be attributed to the uniform particle size and the presence of identical outer surface functional groups [50]. Furthermore, the quantum yield of the red and orange NCDs was determined to be 2.4 % and 8.3 %, respectively. Decay characteristics were examined using the FLS-920 T fluorescence spectrophotometer with an nF900 nanosecond flash lamp. The decay curve of the red and orange NCDs is best described by a bi-exponential equation, as shown in Fig. S2. The decay life was calculated to be 5.04 ns, and 2.06 ns for red and orange NCDs respectively, using the following eq (1). Such a short decay lifetime of NCDs indicates their radiative nature.
τ = A1τ1 2 + A2τ2 2/ A1τ1 + A2τ2(1)

The variation in the emission of NCDs is attributed to the solvent effect, with less intense emission observed in other solvents. The PL spectra of NCDs with different solvents (acetate, DMSO, DMF, formamide) are presented in supporting information (Fig. S3 and S4).

The pH performance of the red emissive NCDs is recorded, as shown in Fig. S5 (a). The red emissive NCDs exhibit optimal performance at normal pH values (6–8). However, variations in PL intensity and peak position are observedd at both low and high pH values due to the protonation and deprotonation process. The orange emissive NCDs show a decrease in PL intensity at high pH values (1.5–3.5), attributed protonation effects (Fig. S5 (b)). Nevertheless, NCDs maintains significantly high PL intensity across a wide pH range, from normal to alkaline conditions. Furthermore, the photo-stability of both NCDs was investigated under an 8 W handheld UV (365 nm) lamp. The results showed that the NCDs remain highly stable to UV irradiation for 80 min, as illustrated in Fig. S6. These findings indicate that the NCDs exhibit high resistance to photo-oxidation, highlighting their potential for various applications. Due to the environmental safety caserns, water soluble NCDs are utilized for metal and biomolecule detection, as discussed in the next section.

CDs exhibit the potential to investigate the effects of heavy metal ions in both solution and solids [51–53]. Here, a range of metal ions (Na+, Fe2+, Ni+, Cu+, Al2+, Zn2+, Ru3+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Ag2+, Fe3+ and, Li+) were introduced into the NCDs solution at a fixed concentration of 1 mM. Interestingly, only the addition of Fe3+ resulted in an alteration of the PL intensity of the NCDs, providing evidence of the NCDs’ sensitivity towards Fe3+ (Fig. 5 a).

To evaluate the selectivity of the NCDs towards Fe3+ ions, fluorescence response investigations were conducted in the presence of commonly available metals ions, as presented in Fig. S7. The data revealed no significant effect on the PL intensity of the NCDs + Fe3+ in the presence of various analytes. For quantitative analysis, different concentrations of Fe3+ were mixed with the NCDs, and the PL spectrum was recorded. As shown in Fig. 5b, a gradual decline in the PL intensity of the NCDs + Fe3+ was observed with increasing Fe3+ concentration. The NCDs fluorescent has disappeared with addition of Fe3+ as seen in supporting information Fig. S8. A linear relationship between F/F0 and concentration (Fig. 5c) was established with a correlation coefficient of 0.9898. Employing a ratio of 3.3 times σ/slope, the low limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ was determined to be approximately 0.97 µM. This LOD value was lower than the acceptable level of Fe3+ specified by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) for drinking water [54]. Moreover, the detection level of Fe3+ ions in this study was comparable to or lower than previous reports, as presented in Table 1 . The quenching mechanism can be explained by the formation of complexes/aggregates, as Fe3+ interacted with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the NCDs in an aqueous medium. This interaction facilitated the recombination of non-radiative excitons through an effective electron transfer process, leading to a decrease in the PL intensity of the NCDs.

Various biomolecules were examined to recover the fluorescence of NCDs + Fe3+, including ascorbic acid, ampicillin sodium, chloramphenicol, cysteine, dopamine, glucose, GSH, l-methionine, arginine, histidine, and lysine, each at a concentration of 0.1 mM, as depicted in Fig. 5d. Among these, lysine exhibited the most significant recovery by enhancing the PL intensity by over > 150 %. The recovery rate between lysine and ampicillin demonstrates that the addition of lysine to CDs-Fe3+ results in a near-complete recovery of fluorescence (approximately 95 %) (Fig. S9). In contrast, ampicillin sodium alone induces a significantly lower fluorescence recovery of around 60 %. Interestingly, the presence of both lysine and ampicillin sodium in the solution leads to a fluorescence recovery for CDs-Fe3+ that is roughly 80 % greater than that observed with ampicillin sodium. Further experiments were conducted in the presence of frequently accessible bio-analytes to examine the selectivity of the NCDs towards lysine, as shown in Fig. S10. The findings showed that the presence of diverse analytes did not influence the PL intensity of the NCDs + Fe3++Lysine, suggesting that the potential use of NCDs + Fe3+ solution for lysine detection. The fluorescent recovery has been witnesses as seen in supporting information Fig. S8. To further investigate this, lysine at different concentrations was introduced to the NCDs + Fe3+ mixture, and the PL intensity was recorded (Fig. 5e). The PL intensity of the mixture consistently increased with higher concentrations of lysine. The relationship between F/F0 and lysine concentrations ranging from 1.25-50 µM was displayed in Fig. 5f. A linear fit was observed within the 0–25 µM range, with R2 value of 0.9872 (inset of Fig. 5f). The LOD was determined to be approximately 0.37 µM. These results illustrate the fluorescent-ON mechanism of NCDs. Upon the addition of lysine to the NCDs + Fe3+ solution, lysine interacted with Fe3+ and undergoes an oxidation process, transferring into Fe2+. Consequently, the NCDs became free and exhibited fluorescence in the presence of lysine. Moreover, the detection level of lysine molecules in this study was comparable to or lower than previous reports, as presented in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of NCDs toward Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The above results highlight the high sensitivity of the NCDs towards Fe3+ ions. To validate their efficacy as an analytical probe, we employed the NCDs to detect Fe3+ ions in tap water and lake water samples. The water samples were collected from Shenzhen University Lake, and the Institute for Advanced Study Laboratory in Shenzhen, P. R. China. The analytical findings for the actual samples are presented in Table 3 . The NCDs solution was mixed with Fe3+ ions at specified concentrations (10, 20, and 40 µM), and the resulting PL intensity was measured. In the real samples, the Fe3+ ions were successfully recovered with a low relative detection sensitivity (RDS%) ranging from 1.9 to 4.3 %. These experimental findings demonstrate the potential utility of the synthesized NCDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions in diverse environmental conditions.

To investigate the dynamics of PL quenching in NCDs, we conducted experiments to analyze the sensing mechanism using UV–vis absorption spectra and decay lifetimes. The blank NCDs exhibited a peak absorption centered at 420 nm, while the presence of Fe3+ ions in NCDs resulted in a red shift in the absorption spectrum, as illustrated in Fig. 6 a. This red shift indicated the formation of NCDs + Fe3+ complex. Upon the addition of lysine to the NCDs + Fe3+ complex, a blue shift was observed in the absorbance spectrum, indicating an interaction between lysine and Fe3+ ions (Fig. 6a). In addition, we compare the absorbance of single lysine with NCDs/NCDs + Fe3+ as seen in Fig. S11. No obvious absorbance peak was observed in the UV–vis spectrum. To analyze the decay characteristics of NCDs, we measured the decay curves under a 415 nm excitation wavelength with Fe3+ ions and lysine (Fig. 6b). The decay curves exhibited bi-exponential kinetics for NCDs, NCDs + Fe3+, and NCDs + Fe3++lysine, with corresponding decay lifetimes of 2.06 ns, 1.6 ns, and 1.95 ns, respectively. Interestingly, no significant change in decay lifetime was observed with or without the analyte, confirming that the PL quenching dynamics followed a static mechanism [68]. The schematic ON-OFF-ON mechanism of NCDs was represented in Fig. 6c. The quenching of PL intensity indicated that hydroxyl or carboxyl groups strongly bound to Fe3+, leading to the aggregation of NCDs in an aqueous medium. This process facilitated non-radiative electron/hole recombination through an efficient transfer mechanism, resulting in a decrease in PL intensity. In addition, the introduction of lysine has recovered the fluorescence of NCDs + Fe3+ by an oxidation process.

We successfully synthesized red and orange emissive NCDs that display excellent solvent-dependent fluorescent properties. The obtained red and orange NCDs exhibit emission peaks with FWHM of 33 nm and 73 nm, respectively. Moreover, the orange NCDs exhibit fluorescent OFF sensing capabilities towards Fe3+ ions, as well as remarkable fluorescent ON detection abilities for lysine. The fluorescence of the NCDs can be effectively quenched by introducing Fe3+ ions in the linear range of 0–50 µM, with LOD of 0.97 µM. Notably, when various biomolecules were introduced into the mixture (NCDs + Fe3+), only lysine demonstrated the ability to recover the quenched fluorescence. The response of the NCDs + Fe3+ mixture to lysine showed a linear relationship within the concentration range of 0–25 µM, with a low level of detection of approximately 0.37 µM. The NCDs exhibited an accurate and precise response to Fe3+ ions, with a low RSD%. Based on these results, we firmly believe that the synthesized NCDs hold great potential as a nanoprobe for future developments in the detection of both Fe3+ and lysine.

Waheed Ullah Khan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Muhammad Muzammal Hussain: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Weiqing Xu: Methodology, Investigation. Yating Zeng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Farid Ahmed: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Hai Xiong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen, China (JCYJ20210324095607021 to HX), the Special Project of Key Fields of Universities in Guangdong Province, China (2021ZDZX2047 to HX).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110507.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
